Remember: Consent is not a one-time event. It's an ongoing process of respect and communication.
Your body, your rules. Bodily autonomy means you have the absolute right to make decisions about your own body.
STIs do not discriminate. Regular testing is a normal and responsible part of being sexually active.
You hold the complete power to say 'No' at any point, even if you previously said 'Yes'.
Menstruation is a completely normal, healthy biological function—never let anyone shame you for it.
Condoms are wonderfully unique because they are the only contraceptive method that protects against BOTH unintended pregnancies and STIs.
Online safety is an important part of your SRHR. Never feel pressured to share intimate photos or personal information online.
If sex hurts, stop. It should always be a comfortable and mutually enjoyable experience for everyone involved.
It is completely okay if you aren't ready to have sex. There is absolutely no strict timeline you have to follow.
You have the undeniable right to seek confidential and judgment-free reproductive healthcare at a clinic.
Puberty looks incredibly different for everyone. Your body's timeline is uniquely yours, and that's okay.
Your mental well-being directly impacts your reproductive health. It's perfectly okay to feel stressed and seek support.
Information is power. Always double-check SRHR facts with trusted clinics or healthcare professionals, not just social media.
LGBTQ+ young people have the exact same fundamental rights to love safely and seek inclusive, affirming medical care.
Emergency contraception (the morning-after pill) is safe, but it's meant exactly for emergencies—not your primary birth control.
It is highly illegal and a violation of human rights for anyone to force you into marriage or non-consensual sexual activity.
Healthy relationships are built deeply on mutual trust, open communication, and unwavering respect for boundaries.
A partner who truly loves and respects you will never, ever pressure or guilt you into taking your clothes off.
Young people living with disabilities require and deserve perfectly equal, frictionless access to all SRHR resources.
Don't be afraid or embarrassed to ask a doctor, nurse, or trusted adult about massive changes happening in your body.
Using correct, clinical terminology for your body parts (vulva, penis, etc.) is empowering and helps reduce stigma in society.
Boys and young men also deeply need safe spaces to discuss their SRHR, complex emotions, and personal boundaries.
HIV is managed highly effectively with proper medication today. People living with HIV live incredibly long, full, and perfectly healthy lives.
Withdrawal (pulling out) is extremely unreliable and carries a very high risk of pregnancy due to pre-ejaculate.
The only person responsible for sexual assault or gender-based violence is the perpetrator, absolutely never the victim.
Every single form of contraceptive has different side effects; what works perfectly for your best friend might not be what works best for you.
You simply cannot 'sweat out' or 'wash away' an STI. Seeking professional medical treatment is the only permanent cure.
Having an unpredictable or irregular period during the initial years of menstruation is a very common biological phase.
Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) can almost effectively prevent HIV if it is actively taken strictly within 72 hours of potential critical exposure.
Your feelings and sexual identity are perfectly valid. Never let intense societal myths dictate who you authentically are.
You absolutely do not need parental permission to demand basic personal boundaries and body safety from anyone.
Experiencing intense period cramps? Rest, warmth, targeted over-the-counter medication, and consulting a nurse are great standard paths to relief.
